items.q: Why don't we have a solar eclipse at every new Moon?
items.correct: Because the Moon's orbit is tilted, so it usually passes above or below the Sun.
items.misconception: There is a solar eclipse every new Moon.
items.random_wrong: The Sun moves out of the way each month.
items.why: The roughly 5-degree tilt means the Sun, Moon and Earth only align occasionally.

items.correct: The roughly 29.5 days between one new Moon and the next.
items.random_wrong: The time for one rotation of the Earth.
items.why: The synodic month is the cycle of phases, slightly longer than the orbital (sidereal) month.

items.q: During a lunar eclipse, what moves into shadow?
items.correct: The Moon moves into the Earth's shadow.
items.misconception: The Sun is blocked by the Moon.
items.random_wrong: The Earth moves into the Moon's shadow.
items.why: A lunar eclipse happens at full Moon when Earth's shadow falls across the Moon.

items.q: Approximate length of a synodic month?
items.why: It's 29.5 days. “27.3 days” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Phase when the lit side faces away from Earth?
items.why: It's New Moon. “Full Moon” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many days in a synodic month (rounded)?
items.why: Exact answer: 30 days. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
